item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't feel like the archived conproc hook was fulfilled, and the interview was too predictable for me. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find myself pretty cool on this one.

I had built an idea while reading the symptoms that those who did not save the dragonfly eventually became the next one, and I was miffed when the interview proved that wrong. Instead, we get basically your standard anomalous punishment disease alongside tons of cryptic rambling, followed by a one-word reveal that, when Googled, tells you the whole story. It's just not a satisfying ending to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So. Let me get this straight. Given a choice between removing 6 more drowning anomalous dragonflies from the water so the damned thing stops manifesting and maybe trapping the last one in a box or something, letting an indefinite number of human bystanders drown while being raped to death by bugs until somebody inevitably rescues numbers 100-105 sooner or later before the Foundation can get to the scene, the Foundation chose the former? And this happened without even a direct order from the O5's overriding the Ethics Committee? Why? What is gained?

I get less annoyed by these things now that the Foundation has so many anomalous ways of predicting the future, but still. This article would go down better if I knew why it's so important to keep people dying from this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Phil Collins urban legend, with the 'Absolutely disturbing' dial cranked up to 11.

Oh Lord, indeed. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The God of the Dragonflies will punish you for your sins, but specifically the dragonfly related ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My newest SCP. Many thanks to Shroomzo for providing critique.

To see more of my work, check out my Author Page!
